channel.title: Bull & Bear With Me
channel.description: Markets, explained without the finance-bro energy. A daily 4-minute read for regular people.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 27, 2026
item.description:
*The market had the best month since forever, Big Tech reports this week, and oil could hit $95 because of a war nobody's talking about enough.*
THE OPEN
April is basically over and the S&P is up 9% for the month. The Nasdaq is up 15%. Your 401(k) had a better April than most people have had all year. Take a second. Appreciate it. Then brace yourself, because this week is the Super Bowl of earnings and the Fed meets Wednesday and there's a war pushing oil toward $95. It's fine. Probably fine.
BIG THREE
- Five of the Magnificent Seven report this week. Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, and Meta all drop Tuesday. Apple and Tesla follow later. The Magnificent Seven is just the nickname for the seven biggest tech companies — they're so large that when they sneeze, the whole market reaches for a tissue. The market has priced in great numbers from these guys. Meaning if they just do "good," Wall Street might throw a fit. It's the 180-bowling problem. We've talked about this. Good is only good enough if good is what everyone expected.
- Oil is at $95 a barrel and Goldman Sachs thinks it could average $90 through the end of the year — up from $80 just a few weeks ago. The Strait of Hormuz, that narrow little water highway in the Persian Gulf that roughly 20% of the world's oil has to squeeze through, is still messed up from the Iran conflict. Iran floated some kind of reopening proposal this weekend, and ceasefire talks are technically happening — except Trump just decided they'd do it by phone instead of in person, which is not exactly the vibe of guys who are close to a deal. Gas prices, heating costs, anything that gets shipped — it all runs on oil. This is the inflation story nobody's talking about enough.
- Jerome Powell is probably done as Fed chair. The DOJ dropped a criminal probe into him Friday, which freed up a Senate holdout to stop blocking Kevin Warsh's confirmation as the new chair. Powell likely presides over his last Fed meeting Wednesday — where they'll almost certainly do nothing with interest rates — and then hands the keys to Warsh in May. New sheriff, same interest rates, unclear vibes.
ONE THING WORTH UNDERSTANDING
Thursday, we get two huge economic reports on the same day. GDP and PCE. GDP — gross domestic product — is basically a report card for the whole economy. How much did America produce and sell last quarter? Estimate is 1.5% growth, up from a pretty weak 0.5% the quarter before. PCE is the Fed's favorite way to measure inflation — think of it like a monthly grocery receipt for the entire country. If either number comes in hot, meaning worse than expected, it changes how people think about interest rates and that moves markets fast. Two big swings in one day. Circle Thursday.
THE CLOSE
Best month in years, biggest earnings week of the year, new Fed chair incoming, and oil still doing whatever it wants. April went out swinging. Let's see if May can catch it.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 26, 2026
item.description:
*Records again, chips again, Meta fired 8,000 people, and cable TV is officially dying in real time.*
THE OPEN
S&P and Nasdaq closed at all-time highs again Friday. At some point we just have to accept that "all-time high" is the new normal and stop acting surprised, like finding out your buddy's been winning at poker every week. Eventually you stop celebrating and start wondering what the hell is going on.
BIG THREE
- Intel went up 20%, dragged AMD up 12%, Nvidia crossed five trillion again, and the chip sector is still doing things that have never happened in market history. We covered this Thursday. We covered it Friday. We'll cover it again because it's still happening. The CPU — the main processor chip in basically every computer that exists — is apparently "reinserting itself as the indispensable foundation of the AI era." That's a direct quote from an analyst, and yes, it sounds like something a guy writes when he needs to justify his salary. But the numbers are real. Chips are carrying this entire market on their back right now.
- Meta confirmed 8,000 layoffs starting May 20th. Eight thousand people. And they're also canceling 6,000 open jobs that hadn't been filled yet. The reason? AI investment. They're cutting humans to fund machines that might eventually do what the humans did. The crazy part is Meta stock is fine. Wall Street loves this move. "Efficient" is the word they use. The 8,000 people who are about to get a calendar invite from HR probably have a different word.
- Charter Communications fell 23% in a single day after losing 120,000 cable internet subscribers in one quarter. Twenty-three percent. In one day. That's not a stumble, that's a crater. Comcast got downgraded and fell 8% the same day. Cable companies are hemorrhaging customers and there's no obvious way to stop it. People are cutting the cord, switching to fiber, using their phones. The cable bundle that used to print money for thirty years is coming apart at the seams and Friday was a very ugly reminder of that.
ONE THING WORTH UNDERSTANDING
Earnings season is running hot right now. "Earnings" just means: companies reporting how much money they made last quarter. Analysts guess a number ahead of time, companies report the real number, and the market reacts to the gap between those two. Right now, the average S&P 500 company is beating those guesses by a mile — earnings growth is tracking at 16%, up from 14% just a few weeks ago. That's a big jump. And it's one of the main reasons the market keeps hitting records even though oil is near $100, inflation is still above where the Fed wants it, and regular people feel like garbage. The companies are doing fine. The vibe is just bad. Both things are true at the same time.
THE CLOSE
Chips on a historic streak, Meta cutting bodies to buy bots, and cable TV losing the will to live. The market's at records and none of us feel like it. Sunday. Go outside.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 25, 2026
item.description:
*Chips hit all-time highs, Intel had its best day since Reagan, and regular people feel worse than they ever have. Cool cool cool.*
THE OPEN
Records on the S&P and Nasdaq again. Intel went absolutely insane. Nvidia crossed five trillion dollars. And consumer sentiment — how regular people actually feel about their lives — just hit an all-time low. So the market is doing great and America is miserable. Love the vibes. Very normal.
BIG THREE
- Intel was up 22% Friday. Twenty-two. That's the best single day for the stock since 1987, which means the last time Intel had a day this good, Top Gun was in theaters the first time. The chip sector is now on an 18-day winning streak — something that has literally never happened before in recorded market history. AMD up 13%, Qualcomm up 10%. If you've got a 401(k) or any kind of index fund, chips are a huge piece of it, and chips are cooking right now.
- Nvidia crossed five trillion dollars in market cap again. A trillion is a thousand billions. Five trillion is more than the entire economy of Japan. It's a number so stupid-large that your brain just rejects it and moves on, which is honestly the correct response. The wild part is it's sitting in most people's retirement accounts through index funds, whether they know it or not.
- Consumer sentiment just hit its worst level ever recorded. Worse than 2008. Worse than COVID. Regular people — not hedge fund guys, not CNBC anchors — are more anxious about their financial lives right now than at any point in the history of the survey. Meanwhile the stock market is at all-time highs. These two things being true at the same time is, to put it professionally, a little fucked up.
ONE THING WORTH UNDERSTANDING
There's a nuclear energy company called X-energy that went public Friday — meaning regular people can now buy stock in it for the first time. It raised over a billion dollars, the biggest nuclear IPO ever. Here's why this matters: AI needs an insane amount of electricity. Data centers running ChatGPT and everything like it are basically small cities in terms of power draw. Solar and wind can't scale fast enough. So suddenly nuclear, which everyone wrote off for thirty years, is back on the table. Advanced reactors, smaller designs, safer setups — money is flowing back into it hard. This isn't your dad's Three Mile Island situation. Worth at least knowing the story exists.
KEEP AN EYE ON
- Fed meets Tuesday and Wednesday. They're almost certainly holding interest rates where they are, but the press conference after is where surprises happen. Keep an ear out. - Visa, Starbucks, Coca-Cola, UPS, T-Mobile all report earnings next week. Good chance at least one of those is sitting in your 401(k) right now.
THE CLOSE
Chips breaking records, Nvidia worth more than Japan, and a nuclear company just raised a billion dollars on its first day out. Meanwhile the rest of us are anxious as hell. Markets gonna market. Go enjoy your Saturday.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 24, 2026
item.description:
*Intel just rescued the Nasdaq from a software bloodbath, oil's at $107, and Meta fired 8,000 people to pay for a robot that writes emails.*
THE OPEN
The market tried to hit a record high yesterday, thought better of it, and backed away slowly like it knocked something over at someone else's house. Meanwhile, after the bell, Intel showed up and fixed the mood. Futures are green this morning. We're fine. Probably.
BIG THREE
- Intel popped 19% after hours. Nineteen. The chip market is already on a historic winning streak and now Intel — which has been the embarrassing cousin of the semiconductor family for about three years — beats on its sales forecast and goes absolutely feral. Nasdaq futures are up because of it. If chips keep doing this, your 401(k) is going to need new pants.
- Oil crossed $107 a barrel. The Strait of Hormuz — the little chokepoint in the Persian Gulf that something like 20% of the world's oil flows through — is still squeezed shut by the Iran conflict. Every barrel of crude that can't get through there pushes prices higher. Airlines are already warning you that tickets are going up. American Airlines cut its whole forecast for the year. Gas, flights, basically anything that moves — it's getting more expensive, and the Fed can't cut interest rates while inflation is running hot, so nothing's coming to save you on the mortgage either. Fun Friday.
- Meta is laying off 8,000 people on May 20th. Not to cut costs. To pay for AI. They're doing the thing where you fire a bunch of humans so you can buy a bunch of machines that might eventually do what the humans did. The people who got laid off probably aren't super psyched about being described as a "strategic reallocation." That's corporate for "we're betting on robots and you're not invited."
ONE THING WORTH UNDERSTANDING
ServiceNow dropped almost 18% yesterday, which sounds like a company must have done something catastrophically wrong. They didn't. Their revenue grew — just not as fast as Wall Street was expecting. Here's the thing about earnings season: it's not about whether you made money. It's about whether you made MORE money than a bunch of analysts guessed you would. It's like if you bowled a 180 and everyone booed because they had you at 195. The score was fine. The vibe is everything. Software companies are getting hit hardest right now because the Middle East conflict is slowing down business deals overseas. Clients are distracted. Budgets are frozen. Nobody's signing a new software contract when the news is on fire.
KEEP AN EYE ON
- Intel gap-up open this morning could drag the whole Nasdaq higher. Watch whether it holds or fades by noon. - Consumer sentiment number drops today. Last read was ugly. Economists expect slightly less ugly. Bar is low.
THE CLOSE
Oil at $107, chips going crazy, Meta handing out pink slips on a Tuesday in May. The market's trying to hit all-time highs through a war and a layoff wave and $5 gas. Honestly, respect the hustle. Have a good weekend.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 23, 2026
item.description:
*Oil hit $104, chips just did something that's never happened before, and Tesla beat earnings — then got punished for it anyway.*
THE OPEN
Markets closed at all-time highs yesterday. Futures slipped this morning. Somewhere in between, oil crossed $104 a barrel, Tesla had a great quarter and got wrecked for it, and weed stocks ripped. Normal Wednesday.
BIG THREE
- Tesla beat on everything. Revenue, profit, margins — all better than expected. Stock dropped 3% anyway. Here's why: Elon got on the earnings call and told everyone the company is about to spend a ton more money on AI and robots. Wall Street heard "spend a ton more money" and did what Wall Street does, which is panic and sell. It's the GE Aerospace move from yesterday — great numbers, bad vibes, stock goes down. Tesla bulls are calling it a buying opportunity. Tesla bears are calling it a company that sells fewer cars every quarter. Both groups are very loud on the internet right now.
- Chipmakers just closed their 16th straight winning day. That has literally never happened before. Broadcom, AMD, Micron — all up 5 to 8% just yesterday. If you've got an index fund, which is basically just a basket holding a little bit of everything in the stock market, chips are a huge chunk of it. This is what's driving your 401(k) to all-time highs right now. Not glamorous, not exciting, just semiconductors going absolutely nuclear for over three weeks straight.
- Oil is at $104 a barrel and the IEA — basically the UN's energy guys — said we're facing the "biggest energy security threat in history." Casual. The Strait of Hormuz is still squeezed, Iran talks are still stalled, and crude is up 2% just today. This matters for everything. Oil feeds into gas prices, gas feeds into inflation, inflation is why the Fed still won't cut interest rates. The guys who control how much your mortgage costs are watching oil with the same energy as a guy checking fantasy scores on Sunday night.
ONE THING WORTH UNDERSTANDING
The FOMC meets on April 29th — that's the Federal Open Market Committee, which is the group of people who vote on whether to raise, lower, or hold interest rates. Right now, a prediction market called Polymarket has "no change" at 99%. Meaning almost nobody thinks they're moving. The Fed wants inflation down to 2%. We're sitting around 3.5%. With oil at $104, that gap isn't closing. So rates stay where they are, borrowing stays expensive, and your mortgage rate stays exactly as annoying as it is now. The meeting is mostly a formality at this point — but the press conference after is where things can get interesting.
KEEP AN EYE ON
- IBM dropped 6% after earnings despite beating — and quietly stopped reporting its AI revenue number. That's a weird thing to stop talking about. - Cannabis stocks ripped on federal rescheduling news. Not legalization — just a tax break. Still worth watching if you're in that sector.
THE CLOSE
Record highs, $104 oil, and weed stocks had a better day than IBM. That's the market in April 2026. Get some rest.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 22, 2026
item.description:
*Tesla earnings tonight, Apple's getting a new CEO, and ceasefire news had futures doing the Macarena before breakfast.*
THE OPEN
Wednesday. Futures were up this morning on Iran ceasefire extension news, which is becoming a daily ritual at this point — wake up, check if the Strait is open, adjust mood accordingly. Markets closed red yesterday. Today's trying to claw some of it back. We're all just Iran-refreshing our portfolios now.
BIG THREE
- Apple named its new CEO yesterday and the market is still chewing on it. Tim Cook, the guy who turned a Steve Jobs eulogy into a twenty-year run as one of the most successful corporate leaders ever, is stepping aside in September. His replacement is John Ternus — head of hardware. Phones, chips, the physical stuff. The problem is Wall Street doesn't want a hardware guy right now. They want an AI guy. Because Apple, which is in about 95% of American retirement accounts whether you know it or not, is widely seen as behind on the AI race. Google's got Gemini, OpenAI's got ChatGPT, and Apple's got Siri still setting timers wrong. New guy inherits that problem on day one.
- GE Aerospace beat the hell out of earnings — revenue up 29%, profits way above what analysts guessed — and the stock dropped 6% anyway. This happens sometimes and it sounds insane but there's a real explanation. We'll get into it below. UnitedHealth went the other way: also beat earnings, stock jumped nearly 9%, and basically saved the Dow from being uglier than it was. If you've got a broad 401(k), UNH probably bought back some of what GE took.
- Tesla reports after the bell tonight, and this is the one. The stock is down 20% this year. Elon has been doing... other things. The car business is genuinely struggling — cheaper EVs from overseas, showrooms getting protested, brand vibes are weird. But Tesla keeps telling everyone it's actually an AI and robotics company now. Tonight we find out if the actual numbers — cars sold, money made, profit margins — match that story. They probably won't fully. The market knows it. Should be entertaining.
ONE THING WORTH UNDERSTANDING
GE Aerospace's thing today is called "buy the rumor, sell the news," and it happens constantly. Here's the idea: when a stock has been climbing for weeks before earnings, that means investors already priced in a great report. So when the great report actually arrives, there's nobody left to buy. The people who were waiting to get in already got in. Now they're taking their profits and leaving. The stock drops even though the news is good. It's like a bar that's been hyped for months finally opening — first night's packed, second night's empty. The fundamentals were fine. The expectations were just already baked in.
KEEP AN EYE ON
- Tesla earnings tonight after close. Margins and car delivery numbers are what actually matter, not the AI pitch. - Kevin Warsh confirmation odds sitting at 30%. The next Fed chair situation is still genuinely unresolved.
THE CLOSE
Iran ceasefire extended. Apple changing hands. Tesla in the hot seat tonight. Wednesday's a lot. Pace yourself.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 21, 2026
item.description:
*Oil's at $89, the next Fed chair is on Capitol Hill auditioning for a job he might not actually have power to do, and Apple just fired its most famous employee.*
THE OPEN
Yesterday was the pullback after the pullback after the record high. Small dip, nothing dramatic. Markets are doing that thing where they're not sure whether to be scared or fine, so they're just kind of standing there in the parking lot, keys in hand, not going anywhere.
BIG THREE
- Oil is the whole story and it keeps being the whole story. The Strait of Hormuz is still being squeezed, a ceasefire deadline hits this week, and crude jumped to $89 a barrel — that's the market pricing in "this probably gets worse before it gets better." We were celebrating $89 as a relief price like two weeks ago. Now it's the scary number. Gas is $4.11 nationally. Your household is paying roughly $190 more a month in energy costs than before any of this started. That's a car payment for some people. Real money.
- The next Fed chair nominee — a guy named Kevin Warsh — spent Tuesday on Capitol Hill trying to convince lawmakers he'll lower interest rates while also being totally independent and not just doing whatever DC tells him to. Those two things are kind of in conflict! The Fed — which is basically the committee that decides how expensive it is to borrow money for a mortgage, a car, a credit card — sets rates by committee vote, not by one guy's opinion. And right now most of that committee isn't cutting anything because inflation is running at 3.58% when they want it at 2%. The Iran war made that worse. So Warsh may get the job and then immediately not be able to do the main thing he was hired to do. Welcome to Washington.
- UnitedHealth crushed earnings and popped over 6%. If you've got a broad index fund — basically a basket of stocks that tracks the whole market — UNH is in there, and it helped offset an otherwise flat day. Apple, on the other hand, named a new CEO and dropped. John Ternus is taking over from Tim Cook, which is genuinely huge news, and the stock slipped about half a percent after hours as people processed it. Apple is one of the biggest weights in almost every retirement account in America. Worth watching how the market fully digests it today.
ONE THING WORTH UNDERSTANDING
Inflation and interest rates are a seesaw. When prices rise too fast — groceries, gas, rent — the Fed raises rates to cool things down. Higher rates make borrowing more expensive, so people spend less, demand drops, prices stabilize. The problem right now is oil is keeping inflation elevated, which means the Fed can't lower rates even though a lot of people really want them to. Every dollar more you're paying at the pump is another reason your mortgage rate stays high. The Strait of Hormuz isn't just a gas problem. It's a everything-costs-money problem.
THE CLOSE
Tesla reports tomorrow. Apple just changed CEOs. Oil is still oil. Get some sleep.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 20, 2026
item.description:
*The Strait closed again, Iran said "just kidding," and gas is four bucks a gallon on a Monday morning.*
THE OPEN
Well. That didn't last long. We were up here Friday toasting record highs and now Sunday night happened. Futures dropped, oil spiked, and the market remembered that geopolitics is just sports betting with worse consequences.
BIG THREE
- Iran closed the Strait of Hormuz again. The same Strait we spent all last week celebrating being open. The market went up huge on the opening. It is now going down on the closing. We got faked out like a cornerback biting on a pump fake. Trump posted "NO MORE MR. NICE GUY" on Truth Social, which is the kind of sentence that moves oil markets in 2026. U.S. Navy intercepted an Iranian vessel over the weekend and here we are, watching futures slide before most people's alarms even go off.
- Gas hit $4.11 a gallon nationally. For context, it was under three bucks before all this started. That's not a rounding error. That's a real number that hits you at the pump, at the grocery store, and basically everywhere money leaves your wallet. Oil running hot means the Fed — the guys who control borrowing costs for things like mortgages and car loans — absolutely cannot cut interest rates right now. Inflation came in at 3.3% last month. The Fed's target is 2%. That gap doesn't close with crude above $90. So rates stay high. Everything expensive stays expensive. Fun Monday.
- Netflix is still down bad, about 10%, and now it has company. The whole market is sliding into the open. But here's the thing worth tracking this week — we've got a brutal earnings gauntlet coming. Tesla, Boeing, United Airlines, UnitedHealth, IBM, AT&T, all reporting. That's basically a full roster of companies that tell you how the real economy is doing. Big banks crushed it last week. Let's see if the rest of corporate America kept up.
ONE THING WORTH UNDERSTANDING
The Strait of Hormuz is a waterway about 21 miles wide at its narrowest point — roughly the distance from downtown Boston to the airport. About 20 million barrels of oil pass through it every single day. That's roughly 20% of the world's oil supply squeezing through one skinny channel between Iran and Oman. When it's open, oil flows, prices stabilize, your gas bill is manageable. When Iran shuts it down — or even threatens to — oil traders panic, prices spike, and it shows up at the pump within days. It's the world's most consequential bottleneck. One country with its hand on that faucet has more influence over your Monday morning than most things you actually vote on.
KEEP AN EYE ON
- Tesla reports this week. Elon's had a year. The numbers will be interesting. - Oil. If crude keeps climbing back toward $100, the stock rally that just hit all-time highs is going to have a very bad week.
THE CLOSE
Friday: all-time highs. Monday: what the hell happened. Same market, same year. Pour yourself something and strap in.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 19, 2026
item.description:
*The Kelp DAO hack just ate $292 million and nobody at brunch mentioned it once.*
THE OPEN
Sunday morning. Markets are closed. Your 401(k) is sitting near all-time highs. And somewhere, a crypto hacker is having the best weekend of their life. We've got a lot of ground to cover.
BIG THREE
- The Strait of Hormuz story just keeps being the story. Iran opened it, oil cratered nearly 10%, stocks hit records, Bitcoin ticked up. We've been tracking this all week — last week Ken Griffin was saying a closed Strait meant global recession, and now oil is sitting under $95 and the market is throwing a ticker-tape parade. Brent crude is still at $95, which is elevated, but it's a long way from the $127 peak that was giving everyone ulcers. The war isn't over. The deal isn't signed. But the world's most important oil pipe is open again and markets are choosing optimism. Can't blame them.
- Netflix is still on the floor and hasn't gotten up. Down 9% after its earnings miss last week — not because revenue was bad, it wasn't, but because next quarter's forecast disappointed and Reed Hastings, the guy who literally started the whole thing, announced he's leaving the board in June. Wall Street treated a 16% revenue year like a participation trophy. The stock might bounce. Analysts say buy the dip. But right now Netflix is that running back who rushes for 110 yards and still gets benched because he fumbled at the goal line.
- The biggest DeFi hack of 2026 happened quietly this weekend and your group chat probably didn't mention it. Kelp DAO — a crypto platform — got drained of about $292 million overnight. DeFi stands for "decentralized finance," which basically means financial products running on code with no bank in the middle. Cool idea. Catastrophic when someone finds a hole in that code. The attacker walked out with 18% of the token's entire supply. Multiple platforms had to freeze operations. And Bitcoin and Ethereum both slipped on the news. This is the part of crypto that the "gonna make it" crowd glosses over. The technology is genuinely interesting. The security is genuinely not.
ONE THING WORTH UNDERSTANDING
The Fed is stuck and it's worth saying plainly one more time. The Fed controls interest rates — the lever that makes borrowing money more or less expensive for everyone. They want inflation at 2%. It's sitting at 3%. That gap means they can't lower rates without potentially making inflation worse. Now with oil still at $95 — well above pre-war levels — prices on stuff you actually buy aren't falling fast. The Fed's own people said this week they're in "wait and see" mode. Translation: your mortgage rate isn't dropping anytime soon. Your car loan either. The Strait being open helps. But it's not a cure.
THE CLOSE
Records on Wall Street. $292 million gone in DeFi. Oil falling. Rates frozen. It's Sunday. Touch some grass. We'll be back tomorrow.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 18, 2026
item.description:
*Iran opened the door, the Nasdaq went on a 13-game heater, and your 401(k) is having the best April of its life.*
THE OPEN
The market just finished one of the best weeks in recent memory and it's Saturday, so let's just sit with that for a second. S&P above 7,100. New record. Nasdaq on a 13-game win streak — longest since 1992, when most of our readers weren't paying bills yet. The reason for all of it is basically one body of water deciding to stop being a problem.
BIG THREE
- Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz open for business and the market absolutely lost its mind in the best possible way. All three major indexes hit all-time highs Friday. Bitcoin ripped. Oil cratered nearly 11% in a single day. Brent crude — the global oil price we talked about last week when it was threatening to end civilization — dipped below $90. Two weeks ago Ken Griffin was saying a closed Strait meant global recession. The Strait is open. The market threw a parade. That's where we are.
- The Nasdaq just did something it hasn't done since 1992: thirteen straight winning days. For context, in 1992, a gallon of gas cost $1.05 and the top movie was Home Alone. The streak is being driven by AI chip earnings, bank earnings, and general geopolitical relief. TSMC — the Taiwanese company that builds the actual chips inside every AI product you've ever heard of — posted a 58% jump in profit. Fifty-eight percent. The AI wave isn't vibes. It's showing up in the numbers.
- Netflix got punched in the mouth Thursday night and is still eating floor. Down 9% after hours after a disappointing forecast and news that Reed Hastings — the guy who literally founded the company — is leaving the board in June. Revenue was great. Wall Street didn't care. Analysts are saying buy the dip though, so if you've got Netflix in your brokerage account and you're staring at it with your stomach in your throat, you're not alone and the pros think it bounces back.
ONE THING WORTH UNDERSTANDING
Oil prices and your daily life are more connected than you think. When oil gets expensive, it costs more to move literally everything — trucks, ships, planes. That cost gets passed to you at the grocery store, the pump, the Amazon delivery. That's part of why inflation has been such a bastard. When oil drops — like it did Friday — that pressure eases. Slowly. Don't expect gas prices to fall next Tuesday. But if crude stays down near $90 instead of $127 where it peaked, inflation cools, the Fed feels less stressed, and rate cuts — which make mortgages and car loans cheaper — become more realistic. One strait. That's how connected this stuff all is.
THE CLOSE
Your 401(k) just had one of its best weeks in years. Oil is falling. The Nasdaq is on a historic run. Enjoy the Saturday. You earned it. We'll be back Monday.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 17, 2026
item.description:
*Ticketmaster is literally a criminal, your 401(k) is near all-time highs, and Netflix just had a rough Thursday night.*
THE OPEN
Markets finished the week green and quiet — like that one guy at the party who didn't drink but still had a good time. Nasdaq's on a 12-game winning streak. We take it.
BIG THREE
- The ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon is the real reason your 401(k) looks good right now. When Middle East tensions flare up, oil gets expensive, gas gets expensive, everything gets expensive, and stocks freak out. When things cool down, the whole machine exhales. We fell almost 10% in late March. We've clawed back more than that since. One diplomatic phone call is doing more for your retirement account than anything on CNBC.
- Netflix dropped 10% after hours and honestly it's a little embarrassing. Revenue up 16%, $12 billion in the door — that's a great quarter. But the guy who founded the company is leaving in June, and their forecast for next quarter underwhelmed some investors. Wall Street is the friend who gets you a great birthday gift and then gets mad you didn't thank them fast enough. The stock will probably be fine. But tonight it's eating dinner alone.
- TSMC — the Taiwanese company that makes the actual chips that power all your AI stuff — crushed earnings. Net income up 58% year over year. That matters because TSMC is basically the factory for every AI ambition on earth. If they're doing well, the AI wave is still real and not just vibes. Good sign.
ONE THING WORTH UNDERSTANDING
The retail investor pile-in is worth knowing about. "Retail investors" just means regular people — us. Not hedge funds, not Goldman Sachs. Just guys with a Robinhood account or a 401(k) they check on their phone. According to JPMorgan, regular people largely sat out this recent rally, watching from the sidelines. Now that the market's near record highs, they're jumping back in. That's historically when you want to be careful — not because you should sell everything, but because when everyone finally decides it's safe to pile in near the top, there's less room to run and more room to fall. Not a prediction. Just something to file away.
KEEP AN EYE ON
- Ticketmaster is now legally a monopoly, per a federal jury. Thirty-four states sued them. They lost. What happens next — whether they actually get broken up or just pay a fine and keep charging you $40 in fees to see a band you kind of like — that plays out over the next few months.
- Oil sitting near $92 a barrel means the Fed isn't cutting interest rates anytime soon. That means mortgage rates stay high. That means car loans stay expensive. The ceasefire is a start. Oil needs to come way down before any of that changes.
THE CLOSE
It's Friday. Your 401(k) had a good week. Netflix did not. Pour one for Reed Hastings. See you Monday.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 16, 2026
item.description:
*S&P 500 hit 7,000 for the first time ever. Meanwhile, a strait nobody knew existed might blow up the whole party.*
THE OPEN
Records on records — S&P crossed 7,000, Nasdaq's on an 11-day win streak — and somehow the whole thing is held together with a Middle East peace deal that may or may not be real.
BIG THREE
- The S&P 500 closed above 7,000 for the first time in history and the Nasdaq hasn't had a down day in nearly two weeks. Eleven straight days of gains. That's the market equivalent of going 11-0 in fantasy and then finding out your opponent next week is healthy again. Tech led everything — Tesla ripped 7% on AI chip hype, Meta and Broadcom announced a massive chip deal, banks posted strong earnings. Genuinely good news. Enjoy it.
- The party has one very large bouncer standing outside: the Strait of Hormuz. It's a narrow waterway between Iran and Oman — about as wide as a county road relative to how much oil flows through it — and right now it's effectively shut down. Twenty percent of the world's oil and gas normally moves through there. It's been closed for over a month. Brent crude, which is basically the global price tag for a barrel of oil, already hit $127 in early April. Ken Griffin — guy who runs one of the biggest hedge funds on earth — said flat out: if that strait stays closed six to twelve months, global recession is unavoidable. That's not a Reddit comment. That's one of the most plugged-in people in finance saying the quiet part loud.
- Netflix and PepsiCo reported today. Pepsi is a signal on whether regular people are still spending — and the numbers were fine but soft. Netflix matters because it tells you whether consumers are cutting the fun stuff yet. No dramatic swings either way. Consider it a yellow light, not a red one.
ONE THING WORTH UNDERSTANDING
The Fed is stuck, and here's why that matters to your actual life. The Fed controls interest rates — think of it as the dial that makes borrowing money cheaper or more expensive. When inflation is high, they turn the dial up to slow things down. When the economy is struggling, they turn it down to get things moving. Right now, inflation is sitting at 3% and the Fed wants it at 2%. That one-point gap sounds small but it means they can't lower rates without making inflation worse. So your mortgage, your car loan, your credit card — all still expensive. And with oil spiking because of the Strait situation, groceries and gas aren't coming down either. The Fed can't save you right now. They're just watching.
KEEP AN EYE ON
- Netflix earnings — dropped after close today. Subscriber numbers will tell you a lot about whether people are still in spending mode or quietly tightening up. - Any update on Iran ceasefire talks. Trump said it's "close to over." Markets believe him right now. If that changes, everything changes fast.
THE CLOSE
Records on Wall Street, a closed oil chokepoint, and a peace deal hanging by a thread. Normal Thursday stuff. Don't let the green numbers make you forget the Strait exists.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 15, 2026
item.description:
*Banks are printing money, quantum stocks went insane, and there might be actual peace in Iran — but inflation's still being a dick.*
THE OPEN
Markets had a genuinely good day yesterday and Wall Street is walking around like a guy who just hit a parlay. S&P hit a new all-time high. Nasdaq had its best 11-day run on record. Even Bitcoin remembered it exists. The vibe is cautiously optimistic, which in market-speak means everyone's one bad headline away from panicking again.
BIG THREE
- JPMorgan made $16.5 billion in a single quarter. Morgan Stanley beat estimates by a mile. Bank earnings are reminding us that whatever chaos is happening in the world, the big banks are doing just fine, thank you. They're basically the house at the casino. The rest of us are the players.
- Quantum computing stocks went absolutely feral. IonQ up 21%. D-Wave up 22%. Rigetti up 13%. One day. For context, quantum computing is basically regular computing's weird genius cousin who's been in the garage for thirty years and might finally be about to do something incredible — or might just be loud about it. A government contract and a technical breakthrough sent the whole sector into orbit. If you don't own any and you're staring at this number with your mouth open, welcome to the club.
- The U.S.-Iran situation is calming down — and that's moving everything. Oil dropped from $104 a barrel at the peak of the conflict down to $91. Pakistan's army chief flew to Tehran to help extend the ceasefire. Trump says it's almost over. Peace talks moving forward = oil prices dropping = gas prices maybe getting less insane = inflation pressure easing. This is the domino everyone's watching right now.
ONE THING WORTH UNDERSTANDING
Inflation is the reason you feel broke even when nothing obviously bad happened to you. When inflation is high, everything costs more — groceries, rent, gas — and your paycheck buys less of it. The Fed's job is to keep inflation around 2% by adjusting interest rates. When inflation runs hot, they raise rates to slow things down. Right now inflation is at 3.3% — higher than they want — and the Fed has rates parked at 3.50-3.75% trying to squeeze it back down. The problem is those high rates make mortgages expensive, car loans expensive, credit card debt more brutal. So it's not great either way. The hope was that they'd cut rates in 2026. Now that's looking shakier. That matters for your 401(k), your car payment, and anyone trying to buy a house.
KEEP AN EYE ON
- Netflix, PepsiCo, and Charles Schwab all report today. Netflix especially — if streaming is strong, it tells us whether regular people are still spending on stuff they don't need. Which, honestly, same. - Kevin Warsh's Fed Chair confirmation hearing hits the Senate on April 21. The next Fed chair will set interest rates for the next four years. Worth knowing his name.
THE CLOSE
Markets are up, banks are rich, quantum computers are having a moment, and there might be peace in the Middle East. Somehow inflation is still the buzzkill at the party. Classic inflation.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 14, 2026
item.description:
*The market almost forgot there's a war on.*
THE OPEN
Green across the board, earnings coming in strong, and your 401(k) is almost back to where it was before everything went sideways. Good morning. Don't get too comfortable.
BIG THREE
- The S&P 500 is now less than one percent from its all-time high. To put that in perspective: a few weeks ago we were watching it drop like a check engine light came on. Now it's basically recovered. The Nasdaq has gone up ten days in a row — its best streak in five years. Tech carried the whole thing, which means if you've got a 401(k) sitting in any kind of growth fund, you've been quietly having a good week whether you knew it or not.
- JPMorgan crushed earnings. Goldman Sachs had their second-best quarter ever. Then Goldman's stock went DOWN. That's Wall Street doing Wall Street things — "good news already priced in" is the finance version of "yeah, but." The bank put up historic numbers and traders shrugged like a guy who just got a raise but wanted a bigger one. Exhausting people.
- CarMax dropped 15% in one day. CarMax is the big used car dealership chain, and their numbers were rough compared to last year. Worth paying attention to if you're thinking about buying or selling a car soon, because what hurts CarMax usually means the used car market is softening. Could be good news for buyers. Just noting it.
ONE THING WORTH UNDERSTANDING
Inflation came in at 3.3% last month. Here's what that means in plain English: a thing that cost $100 a year ago now costs $103.30. The main culprit is gas prices — energy swings around a lot, which is why economists also look at "core inflation," which just strips out food and gas to get a cleaner read. Core came in at 2.6%. The Fed — the guys who set interest rates for the whole country — want inflation closer to 2% before they'll cut rates. Cutting rates is basically the Fed making it cheaper to borrow money, which tends to get the economy moving. At 3.3%, they're not doing that yet. There's a 97% chance they hold steady at their meeting on April 28th. So don't hold your breath for cheaper mortgages or car loans anytime soon.
KEEP AN EYE ON
- The Strait of Hormuz situation. That's the narrow waterway where a huge chunk of the world's oil moves through. The U.S. blockaded Iranian oil shipments there. One Wall Street CEO said if it stays shut for six to twelve months, the whole world goes into a recession. Iran apparently called Washington. Something's happening. We just don't know what yet.
- Jerome Powell is out as Fed chair on May 15th. His replacement has crypto in his personal portfolio. Make of that what you will.
THE CLOSE
Your retirement account recovered. Inflation is still annoying. There's a naval blockade happening. It's a lot for a Tuesday. Go touch grass.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 13, 2026
item.description:
**📈 The Open**
The U.S. just blockaded the Strait of Hormuz, oil hit $104 a barrel, inflation jumped to 3.3%, Goldman missed on bonds, and somehow the S&P is basically flat. Markets are fine. Everything is fine.
---
**🎯 Big Three**
- **The Strait of Hormuz is blockaded.** About 20% of the world's oil flows through that narrow waterway, and we just put a Navy cork in it. Gas was already $4.12 a gallon before this. Analysts are calling it the worst energy disruption since the 1970s, which is a hell of a thing to read before your Monday morning coffee.
- **Inflation jumped from 2.4% to 3.3% in one month.** That's not a drift. That's a lurch. The Fed has two jobs — keep inflation down and keep employment up — and right now both are quietly going sideways. Their response, per Vice Chair Jefferson: our policy is "well-positioned." Comforting stuff, truly.
- **Goldman Sachs had a near-record quarter and still dropped 4%.** Revenues up 14%. EPS beat. But the bond desk missed by $850 million and Wall Street punished them like they showed up to the company picnic without potato salad. The rest of the big banks — JPMorgan, Wells Fargo, Citi, BofA — report this week. Could be the most expensive week of earnings season to actually pay attention to.
---
**🧠 One Thing Worth Understanding**
**The Strait of Hormuz, and why it matters to your drive to work.**
The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow strip of water between Iran and Oman — about 21 miles wide at its tightest point. Something like 20-21% of all the world's oil passes through it every single day, tankers carrying crude from Saudi Arabia, Iraq, Kuwait, and the UAE to the rest of the planet. When that flow gets disrupted — whether by conflict, blockade, or threats — oil prices spike globally because traders don't wait around to see how it plays out. They panic-buy futures and the price jumps immediately, which is why WTI crude went up 8% in one morning. That feeds into gas prices within days, then into shipping costs, then into basically everything that gets transported or made with energy, which is everything. The 1970s oil crisis caused recessions across the Western world. We're not there yet. But the direction of travel is not great, Bob.
---
**👀 Keep An Eye On**
- **Bank earnings all week.** JPMorgan reports Tuesday and tends to set the tone. If they miss, expect the "soft landing" crowd to go quiet for a few days. - **Oil prices Monday night and Tuesday.** Any further escalation around the blockade could push WTI toward $110. That's the number that really starts showing up in everything.
---
**☕ The Close**
Markets are "pricing in the geopolitical risk," which is analyst-speak for "we have no idea what happens next, but we've agreed to be calm about it." Respect the process.
